GDP and Volunteer Count’s Effect on Cleanup Efficiency

An empirical analysis of resource allocation and environmental outcomes

Rayan Tahir, Sriyans Kanneganti, William Ken Li, Andrew Cho

🌱 ENVIRONMENT

GDP and Volunteer Count’s Effect on Cleanup Efficiency

An empirical analysis of resource allocation and environmental outcomes

👥
Rayan Tahir, Sriyans Kanneganti,
William Ken Li, Andrew Cho
🌎

Global pollution has become a massive problem in the world today, specifically plastic waste.

The waste cleanup industry is valued at up to $140 billion in 2026, much of which is focused on removing harmful plastic waste from the environment. According to ourworldindata.org, we are creating 450 million tonnes of plastic waste every year, making it one of the largest waste types. Also, because of the manufacturing capabilities of prosperous countries, there exists an association between GDP (predictor of prosperity) and waste produced.

••• So…

How does a country’s GDP status relate to its dominant plastic type?

How does a country’s GDP status relate to its dominant plastic type? Now for the first question of interest, our analysis is limited to those countries included in the TidyTuesday plastic data set with data from 2019, unfortunately this means that not all counties are represented, meaning that our findings are not globally applicable. This question brings us insight to see the trends amongst higher and lower income countries and their plastic usage. Prior to our findings, we came to the initial theory that we would expect to see a strong relationship between GDP and plastic types, as we would expect a lot more PET, PP, and HDPE types of plastic in higher GDP countries.This is because there is a high consumption of packaged goods and well-oriented logistics systems that focus on consumer-based plastics and items. For lower GDP countries, we would expect to see more PVC and LDPE types of plastic, because there would reasonably be more infrastructure development and lower focus on frequent packaged goods and deliveries, and more on utility based plastics (like PVC and LDPE). We would expect a solid mix of both of these plastic types for the middle GDP countries.

Where did the plastics emerge from?

This analysis used the TidyTuesday plastics dataset along with GDP per capita data retrieved from the API Ninjas API to examine how a country’s economic status relates to its dominant plastic use type. The plastics data comes from Break Free From Plastic (BFFP), a fantastic movement focused on reducing plastic pollution that was compiled and shared by data scientist Sarah Sauve through the TidyTuesday datasets project. The BFFP’s Brand Audits, which are efforts that are led where volunteers collect and categorize plastic waste to identify its sources. The observational unit in the dataset is typically an individually recorded item of plastic waste. For this project, the focus is on the 2019 subset of the data, which has already been cleaned and combined by Sauve. This data is of utmost importance because they provide insight into the types and sources of plastic pollution across countries.

Most Common Plastic Waste Types
Dominant plastic type by number of countries in 2019
Dominant Plastic Type Number of Countries
o 25
pet 18
hdpe 3
pp 3
ldpe 2
ps 1

Americas 35,533 10 107,196,881,908 3.31 × 10−7
Oceania 1,674 1 1,955,118,054 8.56 × 10−7

How Does a Countries GDP and total Volunteers affect their Clean up efficiency when looking at regions on Earth?

Now onto our second question of interest, similar to the limitations in question 1, our second question of interest is also limited to countries included in the Tidy Tuesday plastics data set containing data from 2019. Due to this limitation our data will not be applicable to all countries and will be limited to that of the plastics data set. This question sparked into our minds by wondering about different Regions and country sizes in the data-set. These regions and countries in the data set don’t all have the same population sizes, some are drastically larger than other, this would lead to differing volunteer sizes. Thinking upon this if a country has more volunteers would their clean up efficiency be better? Prior to actually finding evidence, there was a theory, that perhaps those countries with higher volunteer sizes would have a better cleaning efficiency. Now alongside that there was another variable GDP, logically thinking wouldn’t those with higher GDP’s also have better cleaning efficiency due to more money and resources being available? This question was taken one step furthedatar with a motive of wanting to determine if there was an interaction between GDP, Volunteer size, and cleaning efficiency. This question was tied back to one variable, regions. How do regions interact with these variables? Now not all regions of the world have the same population sizes as stated earlier, neither do they have the same amount of volunteers or resources. Because of that there is now a method that could draw us to an even stronger conclusion. As expected regions such as Asia, Europe and the Americas to have the highest cleanup efficiency due to their higher populations and overall GDP’s.

Cleanup Efficiency by Region
Region Total Plastic Total Events Average Efficiency
Africa 176,684.00 11,830.00 14.94
Europe 147,233.00 18,142.00 8.12
Asia 514,339.00 125,818.00 4.09
Americas 35,533.00 11,645.00 3.05
Oceania 1,674.00 623.00 2.69

Alt text: Table of cleanup efficiency by country and year.

H₀: There is no association between a country’s clean up efficiency and their number of volunteers Hₐ: There is an association between a country’s clean up efficiency and their number of volunteers Based on the linear model fit to the data frame, and using a log transformation on the predictor and response variable, H₀ must be rejected based on the significant F-statistic of 40.71 and a p-value < 0.001 which suggests that there is an association between a country’s clean up efficiency and their number of volunteers.
